coredata.dc:title: Lignite-derived nanocarbon as surface passivator and cosensitizer in dye-sensitized solar cell
coredata.dc:creator: Rao, Akshatha A.
Upadhyay, Shanyukta
Narendhiran, Santhosh
Jafri Razack, Imran
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: Interfacial exciton recombination and narrow absorption region are two bottlenecks that limit the performance of a dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC). The present study focuses on improving the solar cell's efficiency by utilizing a lignite-derived nanocarbon that behaves as a surface passivator and cosensitizer. Incorporating nanocarbon enhanced the spectral absorption region of the N719 dye with a bathochromic shift and played the role of a cosensitizer. In addition, the quenched photoluminescence spectra revealed that nanocarbon also aids in the swift transfer of electrons to the conduction band of TiO2 by reducing the exciton recombination and acting as a surface passivator. On measuring the fabricated DSSC under AM 1.5G irradiation with the intensity of 100 mW/cm2, the nanocarbon-based device exhibited an efficiency (ŋ) of 9.02% with a photocurrent density of 20.45 mA/cm2, outperforming the pristine device (ŋ = 6.21%). An enhancement of 45% in the power conversion efficiency was achieved. Thus, the results unveiled that nanocarbons derived from pollution-causing fuel synergistically aided in enhancing the performance of DSSC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignite nanocarbons
Cosensitizer
Surface passivation
Charge dynamics
Flat band potential
Green photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of photosensitizer and platinum counter electrode
Structural and morphological analysis of lignite-derived nanocarbon

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Staatssekretariat für Bildung, Forschung und Innovation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge DST for major research fund DST/ TMD/SERI/ARSS/2020/37(G), Centre for Research Christ (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221. The authors also acknowledge the central instrumentation facility (CIF) Christ (Deemed to be University) for the instrument facility. We also thank DST-FIST for their support through the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). Authors are also grateful to Central Research Facilities, IIT Delhi, Sonipat campus for their support with XPS characterisation. Akshatha A Rao also acknowledges UGC for the research fund obtained from Savitribai Jyotirao Phule Single Girl Child Fellowship. Grant number: UGCES-22-GE-KAR-F-SJSGC-3769.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and c) TEM image of L5 nanocarbon and (b and d) TEM image of L30 nanocarbon (inset image: HR-TEM image of L5 and L30). Abbreviations: HR = high-resolution; TEM = transmission electron microscopy.

(a) UV-vis DRS of bare TiO2, N719-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719), N719+L5-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719/L5), and N719+L30-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719/L30) films. (b) UV-vis absorbance spectra of N719 dye, N719+L5, and PL emission of L5, (Inset: plot showing a bathochromic shift in absorbance). (c) Comparison of PL spectra of N719-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719), N719+L5-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719/L5), and N719+L30-adsorbed TiO2 films (TiO2/N719/L30). (d) Dye desorption analysis of the modified photoanodes.. (Inset is a plot of absorbance at 512 nm and multiple photoanodes and shows that optimal dye loading is for L5-based system). Abbreviations: DRS = diffuse reflectance spectra; PL = photoluminescence; UV-vis = ultraviolet-visble.

(a) JV curve of N719-, N719+L5-, and N719+L30-based DSSCs recorded under 100 mW/cm2 AM1.5G illumination; (b) Nyquist plot showing the charge transfer resistance for pristine N719, N719+L5, and N719+L30 DSSCs measured under dark conditions with a bias voltage of 0.75 V. Abbreviation: JV, current density and voltage.
External quantum efficiency plot of pristine N719-, N719+L5-, and N719+L30-based DSSCs. Abbreviation: DSSC = dye-sensitized solar cell.
(a) Open-circuit voltage decay measurement of N719, N719+L5, and N719+L30 DSSCs. (b) The response time derived from equation (2) as a function of open-circuit voltage.
Photovoltaic parameters of N719-, N719+L5-, and N719+L30-based DSSCs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: DSSC = dye-sensitized solar cell; FF = fill factor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
EIS parameters show series resistance (R
s ), charge transfer resistances (R
ct1 and R
ct2), electron transport time, and charge collection efficiency in DSSC.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: DSSC = dye-sensitized solar cell; EIS = electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

head.ce:title: Lignite-derived nanocarbon as surface passivator and cosensitizer in dye-sensitized solar cell

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interfacial exciton recombination and narrow absorption region are two bottlenecks that limit the performance of a dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC). The present study focuses on improving the solar cell's efficiency by utilizing a lignite-derived nanocarbon that behaves as a surface passivator and cosensitizer. Incorporating nanocarbon enhanced the spectral absorption region of the N719 dye with a bathochromic shift and played the role of a cosensitizer. In addition, the quenched photoluminescence spectra revealed that nanocarbon also aids in the swift transfer of electrons to the conduction band of TiO2 by reducing the exciton recombination and acting as a surface passivator. On measuring the fabricated DSSC under AM 1.5G irradiation with the intensity of 100 mW/cm2, the nanocarbon-based device exhibited an efficiency (ŋ) of 9.02% with a photocurrent density of 20.45 mA/cm2, outperforming the pristine device (ŋ = 6.21%). An enhancement of 45% in the power conversion efficiency was achieved. Thus, the results unveiled that nanocarbons derived from pollution-causing fuel synergistically aided in enhancing the performance of DSSC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A significant challenge in the recent years has been the development of cleaner, cheaper, reliable, and renewable energy resources. A better key to this challenge is converting readily available solar energy to electrical power with the help of a solar cell. Among the promising technologies for capturing solar energy, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have become a viable alternative to conventional photovoltaics due to their ease of production and cost effectiveness. Energy-level engineering, absorption windows available for photon management, and interfacial engineering can all influence the efficiency of DSSC [1–3]. Better cell performance can be achieved by selecting nanoporous metal oxide semiconductors for improved dye absorption, a sensitizer with a broader absorption range, and an effective counter electrode [4]. Furthermore, cosensitization and surface passivation are effective strategies for improving a DSSC's performance. Cosensitization aims to enhance spectral absorption, whereas passivation (the barrier between the photoanode and electrolyte) aids in minimizing recombination losses and injecting excited electrons into the semiconductor, thereby increasing the photoconversion efficiency.

Several studies validate that nanocarbons from different sources can act as cosensitizers with commercial dyes and enhance the cell's performance. An efficiency of 7.49% was obtained using citric acid and urea as nitrogen sources by Jahantigh et al. [5]. Bora et al. [6] cosensitized citric-acid-derived carbon dots to obtain an efficiency of 6.9%. A DSSC fabricated with cellulose-derived graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was reported by Mahalingam et al., with 3.1% efficiency [7]. Thus, from the aforementioned studies, it is noted that common sources of nanocarbon production were synthetic precursors, and most results achieved were obtained through doped nanocarbon. Another approach to improving the performance of DSSC is by passivating the photoanode/electrolyte interface. The most common materials used for this purpose include magnesium oxide, tin oxide, and zirconium oxide. Tehare et al. passivated the photoanode with a layer of MgO and obtained an overall efficiency of 6.05% [8]. Furthermore, with the introduction of the MnS passivation layer, an improved performance with 5.01% efficiency was observed by An et al. [9]. Thus, with the available literature, it was observed that nanocarbon as a passivation layer has not been much explored.

Low-dimensional nanocarbons are a better and safer choice than synthetic materials because of their excellent photoelectrochemical behavior, non-toxic nature, ultraviolet (UV) stability, rapid charge kinetics, and photoluminescence (PL). An additional advantage is its ability to fine-tune the band gap due to the quantum confinement effect and edge effects [10–12]. Due to their outstanding physical, chemical, and biological characteristics, carbon-based nanomaterials have received interest. They are considered strong contenders to change the course of many significant applications shortly. Many efforts are being directed toward creating environmentally friendly and low-cost carbon nanomaterials.

To efficiently produce carbon nanomaterials such as GQDs and carbon dots, various expensive sources that include graphite [13,14], graphene oxide [15], fullerenes [16], carbon fibers, etc., have been utilized. Also, tedious and time-consuming methods such as lithography, laser ablation, arc discharge method, chemical vapor deposition, and thermal annealing [17–19] are used. Beyond all these materials, an abundantly available, economical, and natural source of nanocarbon is coal. The property of having crystalline carbon within its structure can be easily displaced by oxidation and thus results in nanometer-sized carbon nanostructures with amorphous carbon addends on the edges [20]. Lignite, the lowest coal rank, accounts for about 40% of coal reserves. Less expensive, higher moisture content, and spontaneous combustion make it non-favorable to be used as a fuel for environmental concerns [21]. On the other hand, lignite has enormous crystalline carbon domains and aromatic hydrocarbons, making it a suitable source of low-dimensional nanocarbons.

This paper aims to provide a value-added benefit to lignite by utilizing waste-to-energy technology. Lignite, a material known as a pollutant and a fossil fuel, has been used for the first time to derive nanocarbons and use them as a cosensitizer and a surface passivator in DSSCs. Including nanocarbons showcased significant benefits by expanding the absorption window of conventional dyes, improving dye loading, and covering deep trap sites on TiO2. In turn, it promotes rapid charge transfer and boosts photocurrent generation. Thus, this as-synthesized nanocarbon, used to fabricate a device, exhibited a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 9.02%, which is 45% greater than that of the pristine cells.

The nanocarbon precursor used in the present study is Indian lignite mined from Neyveli coal mines in Tamil Nadu. Collected samples were powdered to obtain micrometer-sized particles before treatment. Polyethylene glycol (PEG), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP), sulfuric acid, and hydrogen peroxide were analytical-grade products purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The cis-diisothiocyanato-bis (2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II) bis (tetrabutyl-ammonium) (N719), platisol(T/SP), and Iodolyte HI-30 were purchased from Solaronix SA, Switzerland. All the chemicals were used without any further purification.

Initially, demineralization of powdered lignite was done by pretreating 5 g of lignite with sulfuric acid for 2 h under ultrasonication and washing with water until acid content was removed (Fig. S9a).

A simple and facile method of acidic oxidation was followed to synthesize nanocarbon. H2O2 was chosen as the oxidizing agent. The oxidation process aids in breaking weak covalent bonds and introducing oxygen functional groups. Two grams of pretreated lignite was mixed with 5% H2O2 solution and stirred and reacted for an hour at 100 °C. Further, the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm. After centrifugation, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22-μm filter membrane, and the filtrate residue was dried to obtain solid nanocarbon, as shown in Fig. S9b. The sample is named L5. Similarly, the L30 sample was synthesized by varying the concentration of H2O2 to 30%, and all the other parameters were kept unchanged, and the sample is named L30.

TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized using the procedure reported by Santhosh et al. [22]. Briefly, a known amount of Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP), Polyethylene glycol (PEG), and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were used as the precursors, and the sol-gel method was chosen to synthesize TiO2. The white precipitate was then washed by centrifugation and dried at 100 °C under vacuum. The obtained powder was sintered at 500 °C for 2 h to enhance the crystalline nature. Further, TiO2 paste was prepared by mixing TiO2 with ethyl cellulose and α-terpineol, and the mixture was ground until a colloidal solution was obtained.

The detailed procedure for the fabrication of DSSC is as follows. Initially, FTO glass was cut into 2.0 × 1.7 cm2 pieces and washed with detergent solution, deionized water, acetone, and ethanol for 15 min in an ultrasonic bath, and the FTOs were further dried under air and lamp for 10 min. The TiO2 colloidal paste was coated on the cleaned FTO and sintered at 500 °C for 60 min. After the sintering of the prepared photoanode, TiCl4 treatment was performed at 500 °C for 30 min.

For the deposition of the nanocarbon layer, a quicker spin-coating method was followed. L5 nanocarbon (concentration 0.4 mg/mL) was spin-coated on the prepared TiO2 film and dried in ambient atmosphere before dipping the photoanode in a photosensitizer. This DSSC is abbreviated as N719 + L5. The same procedure was also followed for the N719 + L30 cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of photosensitizer and platinum counter electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared photoanodes were dipped in 0.5 mM of N719 dye dissolved in acetonitrile and tert-butanol (1:1 vol ratio) and were kept for 24 h for better dye absorption. After dye loading, the photoanodes were washed with ethanol to remove the extra dye loaded and dried in the air. The counter electrode was prepared using commercially available Platisol (T/SP) by the doctor blade method and sintered at 450 °C for 30 min.

Finally, DSSC was assembled by sandwiching photoanode and counter electrode, and an iodide/triiodide electrolyte was injected into the device. An active area of 0.25 cm2 was kept constant throughout the study.

In this study, we fabricated and tested DSSCs with and without nanocarbons in two different configurations (LN and NL). For better understanding, the configuration is pictorially shown in Fig. S10. Fig. S10a depicts the stepwise photoanode preparation of pristine configuration, and Fig. S10b corresponds to stepwise photoanode preparation of N719+L5 and N719+L30 in LN configuration. Fig. S10c corresponds to stepwise photoanode preparation of N719+L5 and N719+L30 in NL configuration.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were recorded using a high-resolution Rigaku miniflex powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation source of wavelength 1.540 Å. Raman analysis was performed using the Renishaw model with a 532-nm-excitation-wavelength laser. Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) (using the Thermo Nicolet 370 spectrophotometer) was used for functional groups analysis. UV absorption and diffuse reflectance studies were carried out using Shimadzu UV2600i, and PL fluorescence was observed using Horiba Duetta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained from AXIS Supra instrumentation. Scanning electron micrographs were obtained from Apreo S, Thermo Fisher Scientific with acceleration voltage of 30 kV. A spin coater from Navson Technologies was used to spin coat the nanocarbon. IV measurements were carried out using a Photo Emission Tech solar simulator (SS50AAA-EM) with an illumination of 100 mW/cm2 and an optical filter of AM1.5G with Keithley source meter model no. 2401. Incident-Photon-to-Current Conversion Efficiency (IPCE) measurements were done using Enlitech QE-R. All the electrochemical analyses were carried out using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of lignite-derived nanocarbon
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD profile depicted in Fig. S1a shows a broadened peak at 2θ = 23.8° and 24.1° for the L5 and L30 samples, respectively, which corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon, caused by the stacking structure of the aromatic layers. The broadening of peaks indicated the presence of smaller dimensions of crystallites as well as the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the synthesized nanocarbons [23]. Furthermore, from full width at half maximum, the average diameter of crystallite was found to be ∼0.42 nm and ∼0.43 nm for L5 and L30, respectively, as calculated from the Debye-Scherrer equation [24]. A small bump of around 42° is also observed, corresponding to the turbostratic structure in the synthesized nanocarbon.

Structural studies were carried out using Raman analysis. Fig. S1b shows the Raman spectra with the D band at ∼1362 cm−1 and the G band at ∼1582 cm−1. The L5 sample has a higher graphitic carbon intensity, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.59, compared to L30, which has an ID/IG ratio of 0.62. The defect to graphite ratio shows that the nanocarbon synthesized is of better quality [24]. These nanocarbons can be used for energy-harvesting applications as they contain higher graphitic content, facilitating better charge transfer via π-π conjugation in graphitic networks.

The presence of functional groups in the nanocarbon derived from lignite is identified by FTIR analysis. Solid-state FTIR spectra (Fig. S1c) show the presence of oxygen-containing and other functional groups that act as binding sites for TiO2 and nanocarbon. The broad absorption band at 3000–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations by the hydroxyl groups attached to the surface of nanocarbons. The peaks at 2917 and 2843 cm−1 are symmetric and asymmetric stretching frequency of the C–H group in the nanocarbon. The 1702 cm−1 and 1604 cm−1 peaks are attributed to the carboxylic acid functional group and CC stretching frequency, respectively. The peak at 1171 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O stretching frequency. Furthermore, the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups can also be attributed to the chemical oxidation by hydrogen peroxide. Thus, FTIR results showed that the nanocarbon derived comprises sp2 graphitic carbons with oxygen-containing edges such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [23,25]. In addition, the XPS studies were carried out, and the results further confirm the presence of functional groups in the synthesized samples. A survey scan of L5 and L30 in Fig. S1(d and e) depicts the presence of carbon C1s peak at binding energy ∼284.9 eV with an atomic percentage ranging from 86.24% to 69.58%, and an oxygen O1s peak at ∼530.9 eV with an atomic percentage in the range of 13.76–30.42%. The C1s scan of L5 and L30 was fitted to five deconvoluted characteristic peaks, as shown in (Fig. S1(f and g) corresponding to the binding energies 284.7 eV (C–C/CC), 285.4 eV (C–O–C/C–OH), 286.2 eV (C–O), 287.0 eV (O–CO), and 289.1 eV (CO), confirming the presence of functional groups on the surface of the synthesized nanocarbon [26].

The morphology of the as-synthesized nanocarbon was studied using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (shown in Fig. 1 ). A heterogenous structure with high agglomeration is observed in both samples (Fig. 1a and b). Furthermore, the lattice spacing of L5 and L30 was measured to be 0.24 ± 0.02 nm and 0.26 ± 0.02 nm, respectively, corresponding to hexagonal lattice fringes of graphene/graphitic carbon as shown in (Fig. 1c and d).

Fig. 2 a shows ultraviolet-visble (UV-vis) diffuse reflectance spectra spectra of bare TiO2, N719-adsorbed TiO2, N719+L5, and N719+L30 films. Based on its inherent light absorption property, bare TiO2 exhibited no absorbance after 400 nm. The TiO2 photoanode adsorbed with N719 exhibits a broad absorbance range from 400 to 720 nm in the visible-light region. Interestingly, including nanocarbon in the TiO2 photoanode with N719 dye molecules resulted in a marginal increment in the absorbance range's overall intensity and caused a slight bathochromic shift. This enhanced dye-adsorption phenomenon in the nanocarbon/TiO2 photoanode could be attributed to the π-π and possibly Van der Waals' interaction between the TiO2 and the N719 dye molecules. Furthermore, the red shift observed in the absorbance range could be due to the enhanced donor-acceptor transport facilitated by the presence of an auxiliary electron receptor. Fig. 2b shows UV-vis absorbance spectra for the samples. N719 dye and nanocarbons were mixed, and their absorbance data were recorded. A marginal increase in the wavelength with a red shift (520 nm–528 nm) in the spectra is observed (refer to the inset of Fig. 2b). The increased absorbance can be attributed to π-π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains of the lignite-derived nanocarbons [4,5], and the presence of rich surface functional groups as supported by FTIR spectrum. Furthermore, a widening in the absorption window of N719+L5 in comparison to pristine N719 is noticed. Also, it could be observed from the PL emission spectra that the nanocarbon shows fluorescence in the green region (λmax = 530 nm). This emission enhanced the N719 absorbance in the green region, as shown in (Fig. 2b).

Additionally, the charge dynamics of a semiconductor plays a crucial role in DSSC performance by affecting the photoelectrochemical property. PL characterization has been widely used to understand exciton dynamics such as migration, charge transfer, and trapping. The fate of the transfer of electron excited from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of dye to the target TiO2 network can be understood. A PL spectrum results from the recombination of electrons and holes in the network, and the intensity represents the recombination rate. Therefore, the lesser the intensity, the better the charge transfer and the lower the recombination rate. Observed PL spectra of N719-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719), N719+L5-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719/L5), and N719+L30-adsorbed TiO2 (TiO2/N719/L30) are shown in Fig. 2c. One can observe a broad peak with the emission maxima at ∼565 nm. PL intensity was the highest for N719-adsorbed TiO2 due to rapid charge recombination, whereas nanocarbon inclusion (L5 and L30) resulted in a drop due to faster charge transfer and less exciton recombination. It reveals that nanocarbon also plays a significant role as a surface passivator in DSSC, besides serving as a cosensitizer. Accordingly, the N719+L5-adsorbed TiO2 film (TiO2/N719/L5) showed the highest quenching, indicating better interfacial charge transfer [28].

The dye desorption studies were done by immersing pristine N719, N719+L5, and N719+L30 samples in 0.1 M NaOH solution for 2 h. After complete desorption of dye from TiO2 film, the spectrum was recorded. Fig. 2d shows the UV-vis absorption spectrum obtained for the N719 stock solution and desorbed N719, N719+L5, and N719+L30 dye solutions. Initially, it can be observed that the desorbed N719 dye is slightly blue-shifted compared to the original N719 stock solution due to the presence of the alkaline NaOH solution. Furthermore, a comparison spectrum of N719, N719+L5, and N719+L30 showed a substantial increase in the dye loading with the inclusion of nanocarbon. Amongst the nanocarbon, the N719+L5 film showed a higher dye-desorbed concentration, indicating a higher dye loading. The higher dye loading in the L5 inclusion photoanode is due to the more sp2 graphitic π-π network. The concentration of the dye loaded is calculated using equation (1), and the values are listed in Table 1 .
(1)
C =
A
ε l
where C is Concentration of the solution; ε is the molar extinction coefficient, A is Absorbance; l is the thickness of the cuvette.

As the present work examines the role of nanocarbon and its performance in multiple configurations, the photovoltaic performance of pristine (N719) DSSC, N719+L5, and N719+L30 DSSC in LN configuration is explained in detail here. The supplementary data briefly discuss about other (NL) configurations. LN configuration consists of a TiO2 layer, over which a nanocarbon layer is spin coated, and this photoanode is dipped in N719 dye (refer Fig. S10). The fabricated nanocarbon-based DSSCs were measured under one sun (100 mW/cm2) illumination of AM 1.5 G and current density and voltage (JV) data were recorded as shown in Fig. 3 a.

The pristine cells (N719) measured a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 13.909 mA/cm2, open-circuit voltage (V
oc ) of 0.723 V, fill factor (FF) of 0.618, and an efficiency of 6.21%. The cells' performance was enhanced by adding a nanocarbon layer, as seen in Table 1. It is to be noted that the N719+L5 cell achieved the highest efficiency of 9.02% with 20.452 mA/cm2, 0.749 V, and 0.589 of J
sc , V
oc , and FF, respectively. On the other hand, the N719+L30 device exhibited an efficiency of 7.74% with 18.671 mA/cm2, 0.768 V, and 0.54 of J
sc , V
oc, and FF, respectively. A significant improvement of 47% in J
sc and a 45% hike in PCE for the N719+L5-based DSSC is observed compared to N719 pristine cells. Effective charge separation, faster electron transport, suppressed charge recombination process, and higher dye loading are the key factors responsible for enhanced PCE of the N719+L5 DSSC.

The benefits of adding nanocarbons to DSSC are as follows: it broadens the absorption window, emits green fluorescence, enhances dye loading, and covers deep trap sites on TiO2. The synthesized nanocarbons show fluorescence in the green region, and this emitted green light is, in turn, absorbed by N719 dye and thus boosts current. The other reason for enhanced performance can also be attributed to a higher concentration of dye loaded on nanocarbon-coated photoanodes. Higher dye loading increases the number of photons absorbed by the device and thus generates more electrons. These generated electrons are made to reach the photoanode directly by blocking the traps. Therefore, the carriers are effectively collected and injected into the TiO2 conduction band (CB) in passivating trap sites using a nanocarbon layer. In addition, to suppress back electron transfer and effective charge separation, passivating the photoanode surface led to an increased electron lifetime. This extended lifetime helps electrons to reach the CB of TiO2 faster. Therefore, the N719+L5 DSSC showed the highest PCE due to all these properties.

To confirm the repeatability of data, multiple DSSC devices were fabricated, and the photovoltaic parameters such as J
sc , V
oc , FF, and efficiency were recorded, as shown in Fig. S5(a)–(d). On comparing the pristine device (N719) with the N719+L5 cell, as shown in Fig. S5(a–d), it could be observed that the N719+L5 device showed higher current density, an appreciable increase in open-circuit voltage, and comparable FF. Additionally, including nanocarbon (N719+L5 device) consistently demonstrated the highest efficiency of 9%. The shelf life of the fabricated DSSCs (Fig. S5(e)) was examined for 48 h. After 48 h, a decrement in the performance was observed, mainly due to the electrolyte leakage and not sealing the DSSC.

To validate the performance of DSSC and to understand the charge dynamics behavior [29], the electrochemical performance of the fabricated cells was done using impedance potential with a bias voltage of 0.75 V in the frequency range 0.1 Hz–100 KHz under dark conditions. The Nyquist plot depicted in Fig. 3b shows the resistance Rs, corresponding to the initial value of the semicircle intercept on the x-axis. The first semicircle at a higher frequency represents the charge-transfer resistance Rct1 at the platinum electrode and electrolyte interface, and the second semicircle at a lower frequency represents the charge-transfer resistance Rct2 at the TiO2/nanocarbon/dye/electrolyte interfaces.

Detailed data on charge transfer impedance obtained for all the cells are given in Table 2 . As observed, R
s corresponding to the overall resistance of the device ranges between 3.7 and 5 Ω. The decreased R
ct2 value of N719+L5 DSSC (4.52 Ω) shows that these cells are more effective than pristine cells. The suppressed recombination results in better charge transfer at the photoanode/dye/electrolyte interface, leading to a higher current density of 20.452 mA/cm2. The presence of nanocarbon as surface passivation aided in improving the charge collection by acting as a bridge between the sensitizer and photoanode. This facilitates a better electron transportation path with a fastest transport time of 2.80 ms.

The N719+L5 DSSC has the lowest charge-transfer resistance, better electron transport time, and a charge collection efficiency of 48.8% when compared to all the other cells. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results were validated by the photovoltaic performance of the devised cells, as shown in Table 2.

External quantum efficiency (EQE) is a quantum yield phenomenon that explains a cell's complete charge injection and collection process when measured under a single-wavelength excitation source. Thus, EQE measurements were carried out to understand the light-harvesting properties of the fabricated DSSC. Conventionally, the instrument was calibrated by a reference silicon photodiode. Furthermore, the cells were checked from a 300- to 800-nm-wavelength range. EQE is strongly influenced by the absorption layer's light-harvesting efficiency and the device's carrier collection. Charge recombination decreases carrier extraction, leading to lower EQE. From Fig. 4, it is seen that the N719+L5 cell has a higher EQE percent of 50% compared to the N719+L30 cell (40%) and pristine cell (35%), indicating a better photo response due to cosensitizing N719 with nanocarbon. Furthermore, it also shows that the application of nanocarbon on the TiO2 layer passivates the surface, which reduces the electron recombination, thus improving the charge transfer at the interface. This is consistent with the UV absorption, PL, EIS, and JV studies, as discussed earlier.

Open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) measurements are done to understand the kinetics of charge transfer and the photoconductive behavior of DSSC. From the literature, it is to be noted that electrons behave differently in various voltage ranges [30]. At higher voltages, the electron behaves as a free electron; in the middle voltage segment, one can understand the electron recombination process and electron lifetime. Thus, OCVD characterization was carried out as per the procedure followed by Wang et al. [31]. The fabricated cells were illuminated for 10 s to obtain a steady voltage, the light was turned off, and the OCVD was recorded using the electrochemical workstation. The data were recorded for a time period of 60 s. In this present study, a household incandescent bulb was used to illuminate the cells. The fabricated cells' OCVD is depicted in Fig. 5 a. N719+L5 has a higher response time than does the pristine N719-based DSSC. It can be observed that the inclusion of nanocarbon features increases the effective transportation of electrons, which is achieved via effective charge separation and reduced back transfer of electrons from the TiO2 network that enhances the lifetime of electrons. The following equation can obtain the relation between response time and voltage decay:
(2)
τ = −
k B
T
e
(
d V
o c
d t
)
− 1
where
k B
is the Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10−23 J/K), T is temperature, e is the charge of the electron, and
(
d V
o c
d t
) is the first derivative of transient open-circuit voltage [32].

Fig. 5b shows the plot of response time vs. open-circuit voltage. It is observed that N719+L5 has more lifetime than N719 in the voltage range of 0.2–0.8 V. This increased lifetime can be attributed to reduced charge recombination because introducing nanocarbon on the TiO2 layer enables a rapid charge transfer between the electrode-electrode and electrode-electrolyte interfaces. The better pathway created by the nanocarbon layer on TiO2 resulted in faster charge transport by creating an interparticle connection between nanocarbon, N719, and TiO2. The passivation layer mainly acts as the shortest pathway for the electron to travel into the TiO2 network, enhancing the efficiency as seen in the JV curve and reducing the chances of recombination.

Mott-Schottky study is a plot of applied voltage and inverse of space-charge-layer capacitance (Csc)−2. The entire measurement was done in dark conditions and at a fixed frequency of 1000 Hz as a function of potential. Flat band potential (Vfb) can be obtained from the X-intercept. The following equation gives the relation between the Csc and the applied voltage:
(3)
1
(
C
s c
2
)
=
2
(
V −
V
f b
)
− k T / e
ε
ε 0
e
N D
where C
SC is the space-charge capacitance, ε is the dielectric constant of the semiconductor, ε 0 is the permittivity of free space, N
D is donor density, V
fb is flat band potential, and V is the applied potential. Fig. S6 shows Mott-Schottky plots for pristine TiO2- and nanocarbon-based electrodes. It is found that the flat-band potential for the TiO2 electrode is −0.421 V and that for the champion device N719+L5 electrode is −0.448 V. It is to be observed that with the addition of nanocarbon, there is a negative shift in the flat-band potential, resulting in a higher Voc than for the pristine electrode [33–35]. The difference between the Vfb and redox potential features for a higher Voc is supported by the JV data. It is indeed concluded from the Mott-Schottky studies that incorporating nanocarbon tunes the fermi level of TiO2 to the negative side, resulting in better cell performance by reducing the recombination of excitons.

A comparison table (Table S2) of nanocarbons derived from various sources has been used as a DSSC cosensitizer. It could be observed from the table that for the synthesis of nanocarbon, precursors such as citric acid, urea, graphene oxide, and carbon nanotubes are generally utilized. Shejale et al. [36] devised a DSSC with nitrogen-doped GQDs (derived by irradiating the mixture of citric acid and urea through microwaves) as cosensitizers and obtained an efficiency of 3.92%. The work reported by Kundu et al. [27] showed an efficiency of 11.7% when the nitrogen-, fluorine-, and sulfur-doped GQDs were used as cosensitizers. It is to be noted that an increased performance in the DSSC was observed when a doped nanocarbon was used as a cosensitizer, whereas undoped GQDs/CDs exhibited a comparatively lesser efficiency. However, in the present work, we report an enhanced performance of 9.02%, shown by an undoped nanocarbon derived from low-grade coal that had been only used for burning and producing energy.

In summary, nanocarbons were synthesized from low-grade lignite by a facile and straightforward method of acidic oxidation. The UV-vis and photoluminescence studies showed that the layer of nanocarbon on the surface enhances the absorption range from UV to visible spectrum and also shows green fluorescence. This green emission can be further absorbed by N719 dye, thus acting as a cosensitizer. Based on the comparison of PL emission intensity between nanocarbon-based and pristine cells, it can be concluded that nanocarbon contributes to passivating TiO2 surfaces and reducing exciton recombination. As a result of these properties exhibited by nanocarbon, the highest current density and efficiency of 20.452 mA/cm2 and 9.02%, respectively, was achieved for N719+L5 cells compared to 13.90 mA/cm2 and 6.21% for pristine N719 DSSC. This enhanced performance of lignite-based DSSC (N719+L5) is due to the effect of passivation that aids in reducing the charge transfer resistance from 31.1 Ω for the pristine DSSC to 4.52 Ω for the N719+L5 DSSC. Furthermore, N719+L5 cells' Mott-Schottky studies exhibited an upward shift of 27 mV in TiO2 flat-band potential when compared to pristine cells, which aids in reduced back electron transfer, thus increasing the performance of the DSSC. Therefore, this study confirms the conversion and use of pollution-causing lignite to pollution-free and greener solar energy harvester.

Akshatha A. Rao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shanyukta Upadhyay: Formal analysis, Methodology. Santhosh Narendhiran: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Jafri R. Imran: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision. Manoj Balachandran: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge DST for major research fund DST/TMD/SERI/ARSS/2020/37(G), Centre for Research Christ (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221. The authors also acknowledge the central instrumentation facility (CIF) Christ (Deemed to be University) for the instrument facility. We also thank DST-FIST for their support through the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). Authors are also grateful to Central Research Facilities, IIT Delhi, Sonipat campus for their support with XPS characterisation. Akshatha A Rao also acknowledges UGC for the research fund obtained from Savitribai Jyotirao Phule Single Girl Child Fellowship. Grant number: UGCES-22-GE-KAR-F-SJSGC-3769.
